=== Context Artifact

[[jjsuc_insert]]
//axl_voices axi_cc_claudemd_section
{jjsuc_insert}::
The `MANAGED:JJK` section of the project CLAUDE.md file.
Contains the quick verb mapping table, CLI command reference, and operational guidance that enables the LLM to translate upper API verbs into {jjdx_cli} invocations.
This section is the primary mechanism by which the upper and lower API layers are bridged at runtime.

== Types

=== Identity System

[[jjdt_insignia]]
//axl_voices axt_insignia
{jjdt_insignia}::
A glyph-prefixed typed identity string in Job Jockey's type system.
Every
{jjdt_insignia}
has four properties: a unicode verification glyph (decorative in input, mandatory in output), a fixed encoding format, a monotonic seed allocation mechanism, and optional hierarchical embedding (parent identity encoded as a prefix substring).
+
Two encoding natures, declared via dimension:
+
* {axd_algebraic} — {jjdz_charset} characters, fixed digit count, seed incremented per allocation ({jjdt_firemark}/{jjdt_coronet} under commit lock; {jjdt_pensum} in officium-local storage).
Digit count disambiguates type within the algebraic family, with one exception: {jjdt_pensum} shares digit count 5 with {jjdt_coronet} and uses a `%` sentinel at position 3 for disambiguation.
Case matters: the final character's case distinguishes identities.
+
* {axd_temporal} — datestamp + autonumber (YYMMDD-NNNN), allocated by filesystem enumeration.
No hierarchical embedding.
+
[cols="1,2,1,1,3,2"]
|===
| Symbol | Name | Unicode | Digits | Structure | Capacity

| ₣ | {jjdt_firemark}   | U+20A3 | 2 | 2 heat                               | 4,096 heats
| ₢ | {jjdt_coronet}    | U+20A2 | 5 | 2 heat + 3 index                     | ~262K per heat
| ₱ | {jjdt_pensum}     | U+20B1 | 5 | 2 heat + `%` sentinel + 2 index      | 4,096 per heat
| ☉ | {jjdxo_officium}  | U+2609 | — | YYMMDD-NNNN                          | temporal
|===

=== Algebraic {jjdt_insignia_s}

[[jjdt_firemark]]
//axl_voices axt_insignia axd_algebraic
{jjdt_firemark}::
{axd_algebraic}
{jjdt_insignia}
for
{jjdhr_heat}
identity.
Format: `₣` + 2 {jjdz_charset} characters (e.g., `₣AB`).
Seed:
{jjdgm_seed}
on
{jjdgr_gallops},
incremented by
{jjdo_create}.
Used as JSON keys and in CLI arguments.
Input may omit the `₣` prefix; output always includes it.

[[jjdt_coronet]]
//axl_voices axt_insignia axd_algebraic
{jjdt_coronet}::
{axd_algebraic}
{jjdt_insignia}
for
{jjdpr_pace}
identity.
Format: `₢` + 5 {jjdz_charset} characters (e.g., `₢ABCDE`).
Globally unique: the first 2 characters are the parent
{jjdhr_heat_p}
{jjdt_firemark};
the remaining 3 encode the pace index.
Seed:
{jjdhm_seed}
on
{jjdhr_heat},
incremented by
{jjdo_enroll}.
Input may omit the `₢` prefix; output always includes it.
+
*Reassignment:* When a
{jjdpr_pace}
is moved between
{jjdhr_heat_s}
via
{jjdo_relocate},
the old
{jjdt_coronet}
becomes invalid and a new one is assigned using the destination
{jjdhr_heat_p}
{jjdhm_seed}.
The new
{jjdt_coronet}
embeds the destination
{jjdt_firemark}.
Pace data is preserved, with a note recording the original
{jjdt_coronet}.

[[jjdt_pensum]]
//axl_voices axt_insignia axd_algebraic
{jjdt_pensum}::
{axd_algebraic}
{jjdt_insignia}
for remote dispatch job identity.
Format: `₱` + 2 {jjdz_charset} (heat
{jjdt_firemark})
+ `%` sentinel + 2 {jjdz_charset} (index).
Example: `₱Ah%BE` belongs to
{jjdhr_heat}
`₣Ah`.
+
The `%` sentinel at position 3 disambiguates from
{jjdt_coronet},
which shares digit count 5.
`%` cannot appear in the {jjdz_charset}, so the parse is mechanical: 5-character identity with `%` at position 3 is
{jjdt_pensum},
without is
{jjdt_coronet}.
+
Seed:
{jjdhm_pensum_seed}
in the
{jjdxo_officium}
directory (keyed by
{jjdt_firemark}),
incremented by
{jjdo_relay}.
+
*Persistence:*
{jjdt_pensum}
is a globally unique correlation key.
It appears in `BURX_LABEL` on the
{jjdxr_fundus}
(via `BURE_LABEL` in the {xref_BUS0}) and indexes
{jjdxr_curia}-side
log archives.
Its primary purpose is fossil-record correlation — every remote dispatch is traceable months later by its
{jjdt_pensum}
token.
+
*Heat affiliation:* Every
{jjdt_pensum}
embeds its parent
{jjdt_firemark}
(characters 0-1).
This correlates remote dispatches to initiative work without requiring pace affiliation.
A
{jjdt_pensum}
dispatched during housekeeping with no active
{jjdpr_pace}
still gets a valid identity.

=== Structural Types

[[jjdt_legatio]]
//axl_voices axt_string
{jjdt_legatio}::
A session-local convenience handle for an SSH connection to a remote project.
The meaningful identity is the target pair: (alias, reldir); the alias resolves host and user from a {xref_BURN}.
The
{jjdt_legatio}
token is an
{jjdxo_officium}-scoped
handle (e.g., `L0`, `L1`) that avoids repeating the triple on every
{jjdo_send},
{jjdo_relay},
{jjdo_fetch},
or
{jjdo_plant}
call.
+
{jjdt_legatio}
is NOT a globally allocated
{jjdt_insignia} —
it has no unicode glyph, no persistent seed, and no fossil-record purpose.
Queries against dispatch history use the target pair, not the
{jjdt_legatio}
token.
Created by
{jjdo_bind};
destroyed by
{jjdxo_officium}
absolution.

[[jjdt_reldir]]
//axl_voices axt_string
{jjdt_reldir}::
A relative directory path from the
{jjdxr_fundus}
user's home directory to the project root.
SSH sessions land in `~` by default, so `cd $RELDIR && ...` works across platforms without the
{jjdxr_curia}
knowing the absolute home path.
+
Validation rules (enforced by
{jjdk_reldir_safety}):
must not be empty, must not start with `/`, must not start with `.`, must contain at least one `/`.
+
Example: `projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster`.

=== Other Types

[[jjdt_silks]]
//axl_voices axt_string
{jjdt_silks}::
A case-insensitive kebab display name.
Used for human-readable identification of
{jjdhr_heat_s}
and
{jjdpr_pace_s}.
Pattern: `[a-zA-Z0-9]+(-[a-zA-Z0-9]+)*`

[[jjdt_slug]]
//axl_voices axt_enumeration
{jjdt_slug}::
The category identifier for
{jjsz_gazette}
notices.
Values are the
{jjezs_slate},
{jjezs_reslate},
{jjezs_paddock},
and
{jjezs_pace}
constants defined in the
{jjsz_gazette}
subdocument.

== Records

=== {jjdgr_gallops}

[[jjdgr_gallops]]
//axl_voices axr_record_json
{jjdgr_gallops}::
The root JSON structure containing all Job Jockey data.
Singleton record persisted at `.claude/jjm/jjg_gallops.json`.

==== Members

* [[jjdgm_seed]]
//axl_voices axr_member axd_required axd_property
{jjdgm_seed}
is the next
{jjdt_firemark}
to allocate, stored as 2 base64 characters.
Incremented by
{jjdo_create}.
Serialized as `jjgrn_next_heat_seed` in JSON.

* [[jjdgm_order]]
//axl_voices axr_member axd_required axd_repeated axd_property
{jjdgm_order}
is the ordered list of
{jjdt_firemark_s}
defining heat display and selection order.
Serialized as `jjgrn_heat_order` in JSON.
Populated from sorted
{jjdgm_heats}
keys on first load of old-format files (migration);
thereafter maintained explicitly.
Updated by:
{jjdo_create}
(append new firemark),
{jjdo_archive}
(remove firemark via retain),
{jjdo_continue}
(move new firemark to position 0).
{jjdgm_order}
is the authoritative source for display and selection order, not
{jjdgm_heats}
key order.

* [[jjdgm_version]]
//axl_voices axr_member axd_optional axt_number axd_property
{jjdgm_version}
is the schema version number.
Absent in V3 files; present in V4+ as an unsigned integer.
Serialized as `jjgrn_schema_version` in JSON.
{jjdr_load}
detects migration mode when absent or when
{jjdgm_order}
is empty.

* [[jjdgm_heats]]
//axl_voices axr_member axd_required axt_map axd_property
{jjdgm_heats}
{jjdt_firemark}
{jjdhr_heat}
is the dictionary of all
{jjdhr_heat_s}
keyed by
{jjdt_firemark}.
Serialized as `jjgrn_heats` in JSON.
Serialized with lexicographic key order for deterministic output.
Display and selection order governed by
{jjdgm_order},
not key order.

=== {jjdhr_heat}

[[jjdhr_heat]]
//axl_voices axr_record_json
{jjdhr_heat}::
A bounded initiative with coherent goals.
Contains zero or more
{jjdpr_pace_s}
representing discrete actions.
Keyed in JSON by
{jjdt_firemark}.

==== Members

* [[jjdhm_firemark]]
//axl_voices axr_member axd_required axd_positional
{jjdhm_firemark}
is the
{jjdhr_heat_p}
primary key.
Type:
{jjdt_firemark}.
Encoded as the JSON map key of this Heat's entry in
{jjdgm_heats};
no separate property name.

* [[jjdhm_silks]]
//axl_voices axr_member axd_required axd_property
{jjdhm_silks}
is the
{jjdhr_heat_p}
display name.
Type:
{jjdt_silks}.
Serialized as `jjghn_silks` in JSON.

* [[jjdhm_created]]
//axl_voices axr_member axd_required axd_property
{jjdhm_created}
records when the
{jjdhr_heat}
was nominated.
Format: YYMMDD.
Serialized as `jjghn_creation_time` in JSON.

* [[jjdhm_status]]
//axl_voices axr_member axd_required axd_property
{jjdhm_status}
indicates the
{jjdhr_heat_p}
lifecycle state.
Values:
{jjdhe_racing},
{jjdhe_stabled},
or
{jjdhe_retired}.
Serialized as `jjghn_status` in JSON.

* [[jjdhm_order]]
//axl_voices axr_member axd_required axd_repeated axd_property
{jjdhm_order}
lists
{jjdt_coronet_s}
in execution sequence.
May be reordered via
{jjdo_reorder}.
Serialized as `jjghn_order` in JSON.

* [[jjdhm_seed]]
//axl_voices axr_member axd_required axd_property
{jjdhm_seed}
is the next
{jjdt_coronet}
index to allocate, stored as 3 {jjdz_charset} characters.
Incremented by
{jjdo_enroll}.
Serialized as `jjghn_next_pace_seed` in JSON.

* [[jjdhm_pensum_seed]]
{jjdhm_pensum_seed}
is the next
{jjdt_pensum}
index to allocate, stored as 2 {jjdz_charset} characters.
Lives in the
{jjdxo_officium}
directory as `pensum_seeds.json` (keyed by
{jjdt_firemark}).
Incremented by
{jjdo_relay}.
Initialized to `AA` on first relay for a given heat within an officium.
+
Formerly a
{jjdhr_heat}
member in
{jjdgr_gallops}.
Moved to officium-local storage because each mint committed to gallops, advancing HEAD past origin and triggering the curia readiness guard on subsequent relays.
Pensum tokens are session-scoped ephemeral correlation labels — they do not belong in permanent project history.
Cross-officium seed collision is acceptable.

* [[jjdhm_paddock]]
{jjdhm_paddock}
is the path to the paddock file containing
{jjdhr_heat}
context and background.
Format: `.claude/jjm/jjp_{encoded}.md` where `{encoded}` is the
{jjdt_firemark}
(without `₣` prefix) with each character prefixed by `u` (uppercase) or `l`
(lowercase) to ensure uniqueness on case-insensitive filesystems (macOS
HFS+/APFS).
Examples: firemark `AG` → `jjp_uAuG.md`; firemark `Ag` → `jjp_uAlg.md`.
Computed on demand from the
{jjdhm_firemark}
via `jjri_paddock_path`; not persisted in
{jjdgr_gallops}.
The paddock file itself is created by
{jjdo_create}.
+
Formerly a
{jjdhr_heat}
member in
{jjdgr_gallops}.
Removed because the value is fully determined by the
{jjdhm_firemark}
— storing it in JSON was redundant noise that drifted from the encoding rule
during the case-safe rename migration.
Computed paths match production behavior on every reference; no migration
machinery is required for projects that have already adopted the encoded
format.

* [[jjdhm_paces]]
//axl_voices axr_member axd_required axt_map axd_property
{jjdhm_paces}
{jjdt_coronet}
{jjdpr_pace}
is the dictionary of all
{jjdpr_pace_s}
within this
{jjdhr_heat},
keyed by
{jjdt_coronet}.
Serialized as `jjghn_paces` in JSON, with lexicographic key order for deterministic output.
Execution order governed by
{jjdhm_order},
not key order.

//axl_voices axt_enum_value axd_string
{jjdhe_racing}::
The
{jjdhr_heat}
is actively being worked.

content.text:
I've read the spec, the `jjx_validate` definition (`JJSCVL-validate.adoc`), and how validate sits in the operation taxonomy. Here's my honest assessment.

## What `jjx_validate` actually is

It's a **schema-conformance check**, classified under *Read Operations*, with **no upper-API verb** — there's no "validate" in the Quick Verbs table. The instance reached past the verb table directly into the MCP Command Reference, where it appears bare as `jjx_validate {}` with no scope note.

What it checks (`JJSCVL-validate.adoc`):
- Root is parseable JSON with a valid `next_heat_seed`
- `heats` object present; each heat key matches the firemark pattern
- Each heat has its required members
- **Per-heat**: the `order` array and `paces` object have identical coronet key-sets
- Each pace key matches the coronet pattern and embeds its parent firemark
- Each pace has a non-empty `tacks` array; each tack has required fields; bridled⇒warrant consistency

## Why it gave a lucky-but-unreliable signal

Your instinct is correct. Validate happens to catch the **crudest** merge failures, which is why it looked like it worked:

- An *unresolved* conflict leaves `<<<<<<<`/`=======`/`>>>>>>>` markers → the file won't parse → validate fails at rule 1. So "markers left behind" is caught.
- A resolution that drops paces *within* a heat trips the per-heat order↔paces key-set check (rule 5).

But it is blind to exactly the failures a merge produces most often, all of which yield **structurally perfect JSON**:

1. **A whole heat dropped from one branch.** Valid JSON, validate passes. Worse — there is **no rule checking the gallops-level `jjgrn_heat_order` array against the `heats` key-set**. A merge that drops a firemark from `heat_order` while keeping the heat (or vice-versa) makes that heat invisible to `muster`/`orient` and validate says nothing. That's a genuine spec gap, not just a wording one.
2. **A seed counter taking the wrong side** (`next_heat_seed` / `next_pace_seed` regressing to a smaller value) → future `create`/`enroll` collide with existing identities. Not checked.
3. **Wrong content union** — docket text, paddock, or tack history reflecting only one branch. Not checkable by schema at all.

So "validate passed" means *the result is a well-formed gallops*, **not** *the merge correctly unioned both branches' intent*. The instance generalized the former into the latter.

## The deeper diagnosis

This is a textbook failure of the spec's own **Upper/Lower API separation** (JJS0 §884–897). That separation exists precisely to stop the LLM from "reaching for raw tools instead of following workflows," and relies on lower-layer names being *boring* so they "resist pattern matching." `validate` breaks that contract: it's a vivid, inviting generic word that pattern-matches perfectly to "validate that my merge is good." The agent did exactly what the design tries to prevent — and the name lured it in.

## What I'd adjust

**Wording (cheap, high-leverage — and where you suspected the fix lives):**

- **Scope caveat on validate in the command reference.** Wherever `jjx_validate {}` is listed, add: *"schema conformance only — confirms the gallops is well-formed; does NOT verify merge-resolution correctness, detect dropped heats/paces, or check seed regression."* This alone would have stopped the instance.
- **Explicit merge-conflict guidance in the git-discipline section.** The Multi-Officium / Forbidden-Git section covers *prevention* (orthogonal commits via explicit file lists) and says "confused by repo state → ASK" — but a conflict in `jjg_gallops.json` is the canonical "confused" case and gets no named treatment. I'd add: a gallops merge conflict is an **ASK-the-operator** event; `jjx_validate` is not sufficient evidence of correct resolution.

**Tooling (deeper, optional):**

- **Strengthen validate** to check `jjgrn_heat_order` ↔ `heats` key-set consistency (the missing rule above) and flag seed values smaller than any allocated identity. These are cheap, mechanical, and would convert the two silent failure modes into loud ones.
- A true **merge audit** (diff merged gallops against *both* git parents to prove no heat/pace was silently dropped and seeds only advanced) is the only thing that actually answers "was this merge correct" — but that's real work, not a wording tweak.

My recommendation: do the two wording changes plus the `heat_order` validate rule. That trio turns "lucky" into "reliably catches the failures a merge actually causes," and the caveat stops an agent from over-trusting validate in the first place.

Want me to draft those edits? The wording changes land in the CLAUDE.md JJK section / command reference; the `heat_order` rule lands in `JJSCVL-validate.adoc`. I'd show you each before applying.

What I want to make sure is that the validate mechanics are strong, simple, and well described in your context preparations.  Thoughts?

content.text:
Good — that reframing sharpens things, and it changes what "strong" should mean.

If marker-resolution is mine, then validate is never looking at a file with `<<<<` in it. It's the **backstop I run *after* I've hand-resolved**, to confirm the JSON I produced is internally sound before I trust it. So "strong" isn't about catching parse failures — it's about catching the **consistency drift a hand-merge silently introduces**. That's a narrower, cleaner target than I framed last time.

## The principle that makes it strong *and* simple

I'd anchor validate to one line:

> Validate enforces every invariant that is a **pure function of the gallops data itself** — referential integrity (every reference resolves; every index array matches its keyed container) and allocation integrity (every seed dominates what it has allocated). It enforces **nothing that requires external knowledge** — not intended content, not git history, not "what the merge should have been."

That gives a crisp residual: **green validate = the structure is self-consistent; the only error class left is content/intent, which only I (or you) can judge.** That sentence is the thing I most want in my context, because it tells me exactly what's still on me after the tool goes green.

## Against that principle, the current rules are asymmetric

Today validate checks, *per heat*, that the `order` array and `paces` key-set are identical (rule 5). That's the principle applied at the pace level. But there is **no heat-level analog** — nothing checks `jjgrn_heat_order` against the `heats` key-set. A hand-merge that drops a firemark from `heat_order` while keeping the heat (or the reverse) produces flawless JSON and a heat that's invisible to `muster`/`orient`. That's not scope creep to add — it's the *same rule you already decided to have*, applied one level up. Its absence reads like an oversight.

The second pure-data invariant is **seed dominance**: `next_heat_seed` must exceed every allocated firemark index, and each heat's `next_pace_seed` every allocated coronet index. A merge that takes the wrong side regresses a seed → the next `create`/`enroll` collides with an existing identity. It's arithmetic on data already in hand, and it's the single most dangerous *silent* merge outcome. I'd argue it's squarely inside "allocation integrity," not an extension.

Those two are the whole list, I think. Everything else (content union, paddock text, tack history) is on the far side of the principle line and shouldn't be in validate at all. That's what keeps it simple: the principle tells you when to *stop* adding rules.

## "Well-described" is a deliverable in its own right

Two moves, both cheap:

1. **State the guarantee and the residual** wherever `jjx_validate {}` appears in my context — not just "checks schema" but the pure-data-invariant framing above, plus the explicit residual ("does not certify content correctness; after a merge, eyeball the heat/pace inventory against both branches yourself"). A strong validate is only as good as my mental model of it, and right now the command reference says nothing.

2. **Make validate self-describing at call time** (optional polish): on success, emit one census line — `12 heats, 340 paces; heat_order/pace_order/seed invariants hold`. Then green isn't mere *absence* of error; it's positive evidence I can read back to you. The tool describes its own guarantee at the moment I lean on it.

## Where I'd want your call

The one judgment that's yours: **does the guarantee line include seed-dominance, or stay referential-only?** Including it makes validate the complete structural backstop for a merge (my preference — collisions are nasty and the check is free). Keeping it referential-only is simpler to describe and leaves seeds as a separate concern. Both are defensible; the choice sets where the principle line falls.

What's your instinct on that — and does the "pure function of the gallops data" framing match how you want validate to be understood?

content.text:
Got it — and reading the load/save/persist routines, the mechanism you're pointing at is clear. Let me state it back compactly so we're aligned, then give you a tight desiderata.

## The mechanism, in three lines

- **`jjdr_load`** is the *only* way to read the gallops. It does a **round-trip check**: read raw bytes → deserialize → re-serialize → compare. Non-canonical bytes → `fatal`. The **one exception** is *migration mode* (`heat_order` empty), where instead of fataling it **populates `heat_order` and lets the next save rewrite the file canonically**. That rewrite is your "silent read/modify/write."
- **`jjdr_save`** writes canonical JSON to a temp file, calls `jjdr_load` on it to validate, then atomic-renames. One validation code path for read and write.
- So canonicalization is *already* a latent side effect — it just only fires in migration mode today.

## The key realization

Today `jjx_validate` goes through `jjdr_load`, which means a hand-merged file that is **valid but non-canonical** (different key order, whitespace, a populated-vs-empty `heat_order`) currently makes validate **fatal on the round-trip mismatch** — it does *not* pass, and it does *not* fix. Your proposal is essentially: **generalize the migration-mode rewrite to all of validate** — instead of fataling on non-canonical, normalize it and tell me it changed.

That's a small, honest wiring change of parts that already exist, not new validation logic. And it maps cleanly to your tri-state:

| Outcome | Exit | File touched? | stdout |
|---|---|---|---|
| **clean** | 0 | no | "valid, already canonical" + census |
| **normalized** | 2 | yes (working tree) | "I rewrote it to canonical form — re-check your merge *intent*" |
| **broken** | 1 | no | "can't load/validate — fix it yourself" + first-difference/invariant diagnostic |

The clever part is using *file-changed* as the fail signal: in a merge-finalize gate, "I touched your file" is exactly the stop-and-look event. Two distinct non-zero codes (1 broken / 2 normalized) let me branch without scraping stdout.

## Simpler desiderata for `jjx_validate`

1. **One pass, tri-state** as above — exit code carries broken/normalized/clean.
2. **The fix is *only* normalization** (key order, whitespace, `heat_order` population). It never invents or repairs missing/contradictory data — structural breakage is exit 1, never a silent "fix."
3. **Idempotent**: re-running after a `2` yields `0`. That's the proof the fix took.
4. **Self-describing stdout**: every outcome names its bucket + prints a census, so green is positive evidence.
5. **Well-described in my context**: the command-reference entry states the three outcomes and the residual — *"normalized ≠ semantically correct; eyeball the heat/pace inventory yourself."*

## The one decision I need from you

**On a `normalized` (exit 2), does validate commit the rewrite, or only write the working tree?** My lean: **working-tree only, no commit** — because *you/I* own the merge commit, and a surprise journalled commit mid-merge violates "Claude is additive, operator decides." But that breaks validate's current read-only purity (it becomes a writer that takes the lock and mutates), so it's genuinely your call.

## Proposed next action

Once you settle commit-or-not: **slate one pace** in a JJK heat whose docket is *"Recast `jjx_validate` as a normalize-and-report pass (tri-state exit; normalization-only fix; working-tree write per your decision; self-describing stdout), and update the JJK command-reference wording to state the three outcomes and the residual."* Single, mechanical-ish pace — no new validation rules, just rewiring + wording.

Want to make the commit-or-not call, and should I slate it?

Yes — that rationale holds, on both legs:

- **Additive safety**: a normalizing commit is never destructive; a wrong one is fixed by the next commit. Consistent with the "Claude is additive" discipline.
- **Future separate repo**: once the gallops lives in its own repo, validate's normalization churn is self-contained — it doesn't muddy the host project's history. That actually *strengthens* the case for committing, since the worry about commit noise evaporates.

I'm convinced. Commit-on-normalize it is.

Two consequences that choice forces — worth locking now so they're not a mount-time surprise, not worth deciding in depth:

1. **validate is gallops-wide, so it can't use the standard persist path.** `jjdr_persist` requires a heat/pace identity to derive its action code; a whole-file normalization is affiliated with neither. So validate needs its own commit path — a new action code and either a gallops-level commit or the marker-style primitive. That's a real spec touch, not just wiring.

2. **Committing while a git merge is unfinished finalizes the merge.** Given the whole point is post-merge cleanup, I think that's *welcome* — validate becomes the thing that lands a gallops merge with a clean canonical tree. But the commit primitive has to behave correctly with `MERGE_HEAD` present (consume it, two parents), so "correct under in-progress merge" is a constraint to verify, not assume.

Both of those are locked-decision material for the docket, and they keep the pace from looking deceptively trivial.

One thing I need to actually slate: **which heat?** A validate-API recast feels at home in **₣BD (jjk-v4-1-fast-tweaks)** since it's racing and small-grained — but it's a touch more than a "fast tweak," so you might want it in ₣BF (jjk-v4-2-add-tackle) or its own home. Tell me the firemark and I'll slate it with commit-on-normalize, the tri-state, and those two constraints captured.

# jjezs_slate scout-paddock-per-heat

## Character
Intricate but mechanical — restructure one search loop; rendering decision belongs to mount.

## What done looks like
`scout` (`jjx_search`) surfaces a heat whose paddock matches the pattern **even when the heat has zero paces**. Today the entire search — including the paddock read — is nested inside the per-pace loop (`for coronet_key in &heat.order`), so a pace-less heat's `order` is empty, the loop body never runs, and the paddock is never read. Planning/design heats, whose whole value lives in the paddock, are exactly this case and are silently invisible.

The bug is broader than zero-pace heats: the paddock sits in the `else` of an `if silks / else if text / else if direction / else paddock` chain, so a heat whose paces all self-match never has its paddock searched either; and when searched, the paddock is re-read from disk once per pace.

## The fix
Hoist the paddock search out of the pace loop into a **once-per-heat** search, independent of pace count. When only the paddock matches, emit a **heat-level row** (heat header + paddock excerpt, no coronet).

## Locked constraints
- Paddock search is per-heat, not per-pace; paddock read happens once per heat.
- Paddock-only matches render as a heat-level row with no coronet.
- The `actionable` filter applies only to pace rows — a paddock is not actionable, so `actionable` suppresses heat-level paddock rows.
- No status filter regression: all heats (racing, stabled, retired) remain searched.

## Sources
`Tools/jjk/vov_veiled/src/jjrsc_scout.rs` (the nested loop and the else-chain). Spook origin: ₣BH (rbk-29-win-enshrine-triple), a 0-pace heat whose paddock saturates with `enshrine`/`reliquary`/`unify` yet never appeared in scout.
